title: Ephesians 4
subtitle: Unity of the Body and the New Self

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — "One Body, One Spirit, One Hope"

heb.paragraph: The noun henotēs appears only twice in the NT, both in Ephesians (4:3, 13). The ‘unity of the Spirit’ (v. 3) is something believers are to ‘keep’ (tērein), not create: it already exists as the Spirit’s work, and the community’s task is to maintain it. The seven ‘ones’ (vv. 4–6)—one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God—provide the theological foundation for this unity.

The noun katartismos (v. 12) denotes the process of equipping or fitting out. Christ gave gifted leaders ‘to equip his people for works of service.’ The critical exegetical question is the punctuation of v. 12: some translations place a comma after ‘saints’ (making equipping, service, and building up three parallel purposes), while others (following the Greek more naturally) read equipping the saints as the purpose, with works of service and building up the body as the result.

refs.note: Paul’s extended treatment of spiritual gifts and the body metaphor in 1 Corinthians provides the closest parallel to Eph 4:7–16, though Ephesians focuses on leadership gifts while 1 Corinthians covers a broader range.

Paul quotes Psalm 68:18 in Eph 4:8 but adapts it: the psalm reads ‘you received gifts from people,’ while Paul writes ‘he gave gifts to his people.’ The adaptation reflects Christ’s ascension theology: having ascended victoriously, he distributes the spoils.

notes.note: Paul’s call to ‘walk worthy’ (peripatein axiōs) connects the ethical imperative to the theological indicative of chs. 1–3. The virtues listed are the practical requirements for maintaining the unity the Spirit has established. MacArthur notes that these are not natural virtues but Spirit-produced dispositions.

The seven ‘ones’ are a creedal summary: one body (the church), one Spirit (the Holy Spirit), one hope (eschatological expectation), one Lord (Christ), one faith (the apostolic gospel), one baptism (initiation into Christ), one God and Father (the source and goal of all). This sevenfold unity is the theological ground of the ethical call.

Christ’s ascension gifts are leadership roles designed to equip the saints for ministry. The four (or five) gifted offices—apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors and teachers—are not the ministers who do the ministry but the equippers who prepare the whole body for service.

The goal of maturity is corporate, not just individual: ‘until we all reach unity in the faith and in the knowledge of the Son of God and become mature.’ The body metaphor emphasises that growth happens as each part does its work: the body builds itself up in love.

Calvin reads the seven ‘ones’ as the non-negotiable foundations of Christian unity. Diversity of opinion on secondary matters is permissible; division on these seven is schism. Calvin notes that ‘one faith’ does not mean identical understanding of every doctrine but shared allegiance to the apostolic gospel.

Calvin emphasises that the ministry belongs to the whole body, not to a clerical elite. Pastors and teachers equip the saints, and the saints do the work of ministry. The goal is maturity, measured by ‘the whole measure of the fullness of Christ’ (v. 13)—Christlikeness in the community, not just in individuals.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The quotation of Ps 68:18 involves a significant textual adaptation: the MT reads ‘you received’ (laqahta) while Paul writes ‘he gave’ (edoken). The NET translators note that the Aramaic targum also reads ‘gave,’ suggesting Paul may follow an established interpretive tradition.

The phrase eis ta katōtera merē tēs gēs (into the lower parts of the earth) is debated: it could refer to the incarnation (descent to earth), the burial (descent to the grave), or the descent to Hades. The NET favours the incarnation reading.

lincoln.source: Andrew T. Lincoln, Ephesians, WBC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lincoln identifies the seven ‘ones’ as a pre-Pauline confessional formula adapted for the letter’s purposes. The passage’s argument moves from the indicative of unity (vv. 4–6) to the diversity of gifts (vv. 7–11) to the goal of maturity (vv. 12–16). Lincoln reads the body metaphor (vv. 15–16) as organic and dynamic: the church is not a static institution but a growing organism directed by Christ as head.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Ephesians, Pillar (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien reads the paraenesis as grounded in the mystery of ch. 3: the unity Paul calls for is the lived expression of the mystery of Jew-Gentile co-incorporation. The seven ‘ones’ provide the theological basis, the ascension gifts provide the means, and corporate maturity is the goal. O’Brien notes that the ‘fullness of Christ’ (v. 13) echoes the fullness language of 1:23 and 3:19, creating a lexical thread through the letter.

hist.context: Ephesians 4 marks the transition from doctrine (chs. 1–3) to ethics (chs. 4–6), parallel to the structure of Romans (chs. 1–11 / 12–16) and Galatians (chs. 1–4 / 5–6). Paul’s exhortation begins with the call to ‘live a life worthy of the calling you have received’ (v. 1)—the therefore of the letter. The virtues listed in vv. 2–3 (humility, gentleness, patience, bearing with one another in love) are not abstract qualities but the concrete dispositions required for maintaining unity. The seven ‘ones’ (vv. 4–6) form a confessional statement grounding unity in trinitarian theology. The diversity-within-unity theme continues in vv. 7–16: Christ ascended and gave gifts (apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors/teachers) for the purpose of building up the body toward maturity. The body metaphor (vv. 15–16) emphasises organic growth, mutual dependence, and Christ as the head from whom the whole body is coordinated.

heb.gloss: old self, old humanity, former person
heb.paragraph: The phrase palaios anthrōpos (v. 22) denotes the pre-conversion self characterised by the corruption of deceitful desires. The corresponding ‘new self’ (kainos anthrōpos, v. 24) is ‘created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness.’ The language of ‘putting off’ and ‘putting on’ draws on the clothing metaphor, suggesting a decisive change of identity rather than gradual improvement.

refs.note: Paul’s call to be ‘transformed by the renewing of your mind’ parallels the renewal language of Eph 4:23, with both passages locating ethical transformation in the renewal of the mind.
The parallel passage in Colossians uses the same put-off/put-on language: ‘you have taken off your old self ... and have put on the new self.’

notes.note: The pagan way of life is characterised by intellectual futility, moral darkness, spiritual alienation, and sensual indulgence. MacArthur notes the downward progression: darkened understanding leads to ignorance, ignorance to hardened hearts, hardened hearts to insensitivity, insensitivity to sensuality and greed.

The put-off/put-on language describes the believer’s radical identity change. The old self is not reformed but discarded; the new self is not improved but created. The renewal of the mind (v. 23) is the mechanism: right thinking produces right living.

Calvin reads the description of pagan darkness as a portrait of total depravity in its moral manifestation. The problem is not merely bad behaviour but darkened understanding: sin begins in the mind and corrupts all subsequent action. Calvin emphasises the progressive nature of the descent: each stage opens the door to the next.

Calvin explains the put-off/put-on metaphor as a description of the definitive (positional) and progressive (experiential) aspects of sanctification. The old self was put off at conversion; the new self is put on in daily practice. The renewal of the mind is the bridge between the two.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The aorist infinitive apothesthai (to put off) denotes a decisive action, not a gradual process. The NET translators note that this may reflect the once-for-all character of the conversion event.

The phrase kata theon (according to God, like God) indicates that the new self is patterned after God’s own character. The virtues ‘righteousness and holiness’ are qualified by ‘of the truth’ (tēs alētheias), distinguishing genuine, God-derived righteousness from mere human morality.

lincoln.source: Andrew T. Lincoln, Ephesians, WBC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lincoln identifies the passage as a baptismal catechesis: the put-off/put-on framework reflects early Christian instruction associated with the rite of baptism. The renewal of the mind (v. 23) is the hermeneutical key: ethical transformation begins with a reoriented perspective shaped by ‘the truth that is in Jesus’ (v. 21).

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Ephesians, Pillar (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien notes the contrast structure: ‘no longer walk as the Gentiles walk’ (v. 17) introduces the negative (vv. 17–19), and ‘that is not the way you learned Christ’ (v. 20) introduces the positive (vv. 20–24). The phrase ‘learned Christ’ is remarkable: Christ is not merely the subject of learning but its content, context, and goal.

hist.context: Paul contrasts the believers’ new way of life with the futile thinking of the Gentile world (vv. 17–19). The description of pagan moral blindness—‘darkened in their understanding, separated from the life of God because of the ignorance that is in them due to the hardening of their hearts’ (v. 18)—provides the negative backdrop against which the positive instruction is set. The believers, however, have ‘learned Christ’ (v. 20)—an unusual expression suggesting that Christ is both the content and the context of their ethical formation. The put-off/put-on framework (vv. 22–24) summarises the ethical transition: the old self is discarded, the mind is renewed, and the new self is assumed.

sections.header: Verses 25–32 — "Speaking Truth, Doing Good, Forgiving Others"

heb.paragraph: The verb lypeō in v. 30 is applied to the Holy Spirit: ‘do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God.’ The personification implies that the Spirit is a person who can be affected by the behaviour of believers. Sin does not merely violate a rule but causes sorrow to the indwelling Spirit, damaging the relational bond between believer and God.

refs.note: The parallel passage in Colossians provides a nearly identical list of virtues and concludes with the same christological basis for forgiveness: ‘Forgive as the Lord forgave you.’
The quotation in Eph 4:25—‘speak truthfully to your neighbour’—is drawn from Zechariah’s vision of the restored community, applying the prophetic ethic to the new covenant community.

notes.note: Each specific instruction has a positive and negative component. Falsehood is replaced by truthfulness, sinful anger by self-control, stealing by productive work and generosity. MacArthur notes that the positive commands go beyond prohibition: the thief is not merely told to stop stealing but to work with his own hands and share with those in need.

Speech is singled out because words shape community. ‘Unwholesome talk’ (logos sapros) literally means ‘rotten speech.’ The replacement is speech that ‘builds others up according to their needs.’ Grieving the Holy Spirit (v. 30) is presented as the consequence of corrupt speech and relational sin. The forgiveness imperative (v. 32) is grounded in the pattern of divine forgiveness in Christ.

Calvin carefully distinguishes between righteous indignation and sinful anger. ‘Be angry and do not sin’ (quoting Ps 4:4) permits moral anger at injustice but forbids anger that festers, escalates, or becomes vindictive. The command not to let the sun go down on anger is practical wisdom: unresolved anger gives the devil a foothold.

Calvin reads the command not to grieve the Spirit as the most searching motivation for holiness: sin is not merely a violation of law but a personal affront to the indwelling Spirit who sealed the believer for the day of redemption. The forgiveness imperative is not optional but flows from the gospel’s logic: those who have been forgiven an infinite debt cannot withhold forgiveness from others.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The imperative orgizesthe (be angry) followed by the prohibition mē hamartanete (do not sin) reflects the LXX of Ps 4:4. The NET translators note that the first imperative may be concessive: ‘even if you are angry, do not sin.’

The aorist imperative mē lypeite (do not grieve) with the present-time focus indicates that this is a standing command. The phrase eis hēmeran apolytrōseōs (for the day of redemption) connects the Spirit’s sealing to eschatological consummation.

lincoln.source: Andrew T. Lincoln, Ephesians, WBC (1990) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Lincoln identifies these as community-shaping instructions that concretise the put-off/put-on framework. Each instruction is directed toward the health of the body (note the repeated ‘members of one body’ language). Lincoln reads the imperative to forgive ‘as God in Christ forgave you’ as the ethical summary of the chapter: the community’s life is to mirror the divine pattern of grace.

obrien.source: Peter T. O'Brien, Ephesians, Pillar (1999) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: O’Brien notes that the specific instructions are not random but cluster around the theme of community life: truth-telling, anger management, honest work, edifying speech, and forgiveness are all essential for the body’s healthy functioning. The christological basis of v. 32 provides the theological ground: ethics are not autonomous but derive from the gospel narrative.

hist.context: Paul unpacks the put-off/put-on framework in specific ethical instructions. The section moves through a series of contrasts: falsehood vs. truth-telling (v. 25), sinful anger vs. controlled anger (vv. 26–27), stealing vs. working and sharing (v. 28), corrupt speech vs. edifying speech (v. 29), grieving the Spirit vs. living in the Spirit (v. 30), bitterness and rage vs. kindness and forgiveness (vv. 31–32). The motivation for forgiveness is christological: ‘forgive each other, just as in Christ God forgave you’ (v. 32). The pattern of Christ’s self-giving becomes the norm for community life.

rows.label: vv.1–3
rows.text: Walk worthy: humility, gentleness, patience, maintaining unity

rows.label: vv.4–6
rows.text: Seven ‘ones’: one body, Spirit, hope, Lord, faith, baptism, God

rows.label: vv.7–11
rows.text: Ascension gifts: apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors/teachers

rows.label: vv.12–16
rows.text: Equipping for maturity: the body grows as each part does its work

rows.label: vv.17–19
rows.text: No longer live as the Gentiles: futile thinking and moral darkness

rows.label: vv.20–24
rows.text: Learned Christ: put off the old self, put on the new

rows.label: vv.25–28
rows.text: Specific ethics: truth, anger, work, generosity

rows.label: vv.29–32
rows.text: Speech, grieving the Spirit, forgiveness as God forgave

lit.note: Ephesians 4 transitions from doctrine to ethics with the call to walk worthy (v. 1). The chapter establishes the theological basis for unity (seven ‘ones’), the gifted leadership for maturity (ascension gifts), the framework for moral transformation (put off/put on), and the specific virtues that build community.

themes.note: Holiness dominates as the chapter moves from theological foundations to ethical imperatives. Faith and Mission are prominent through the unity of faith and the gifting of the church for service. Mercy appears through the forgiveness imperative grounded in Christ’s pattern.

hebtext.note: The noun henotēs appears only twice in the NT, both in Ephesians (4:3, 13). The ‘unity of the Spirit’ (v. 3) is something believers are to ‘keep’ (tērein), not create: it already exists as the Spirit’s work, and the community’s task is to maintain it. The seven ‘ones’ (vv. 4–6)—one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God—provide the theological foundation for this unity.

The noun katartismos (v. 12) denotes the process of equipping or fitting out. Christ gave gifted leaders ‘to equip his people for works of service.’ The critical exegetical question is the punctuation of v. 12: some translations place a comma after ‘saints’ (making equipping, service, and building up three parallel purposes), while others (following the Greek more naturally) read equipping the saints as the purpose, with works of service and building up the body as the result.

hebtext.gloss: old self, old humanity, former person
hebtext.note: The phrase palaios anthrōpos (v. 22) denotes the pre-conversion self characterised by the corruption of deceitful desires. The corresponding ‘new self’ (kainos anthrōpos, v. 24) is ‘created to be like God in true righteousness and holiness.’ The language of ‘putting off’ and ‘putting on’ draws on the clothing metaphor, suggesting a decisive change of identity rather than gradual improvement.

The verb lypeō in v. 30 is applied to the Holy Spirit: ‘do not grieve the Holy Spirit of God.’ The personification implies that the Spirit is a person who can be affected by the behaviour of believers. Sin does not merely violate a rule but causes sorrow to the indwelling Spirit, damaging the relational bond between believer and God.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ
ppl.text: 7–16: Christ ascended and gave gifts (apostles, prophets, evangelists, pastors/teachers) for the purpose of building up the body toward maturity…

trans.title: Comparing translations in Unity of the Body and the New Self

translations.text: (What does “he ascended” mean except that he also descended to the lower, earthly regions?
(In saying, “He ascended,” what does it mean but that he had also descended into the lower regions, the earth?
As a prisoner for the Lord, then, I urge you to live a life worthy of the calling you have received.
I therefore, a prisoner for the Lord, urge you to walk in a manner worthy of the calling to which you have been called,

discourse.thesis: Walk worthy of your calling — in unity, maturity, and transformed character — putting off the old self and putting on the new self created after God's likeness.

nodes.marker: I therefore urge you (Παρακαλῶ οὖν ὑμᾶς)
nodes.text: The ethical pivot of the letter: walk worthy (ἀξίως περιπατῆσαι) of the calling to which you have been called. The 'therefore' (οὖν) bridges the doctrinal (chs. 1-3) and ethical (chs. 4-6) halves — the imperative is grounded in the indicative. The character virtues: humility, gentleness, patience, bearing with one another in love. The aim: maintaining the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace.

The sevenfold foundation of Christian unity: one body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father of all who is over all and through all and in all. The theological basis for ethical unity — not organizational conformity but participation in one trinitarian reality.

children.text: The Trinitarian structure: Spirit (v. 4), Lord/Son (v. 5), Father (v. 6). The sevenfold unity is a liturgical creed — likely echoing early Christian catechesis. To fragment the church is to act against the grain of the Triune God's own nature.

nodes.text: Grace was given to each according to Christ's measure (Ps 68:18 cited to establish Christ's authority to distribute gifts). Christ gave gifts: apostles, prophets, evangelists, shepherds and teachers — all for equipping the saints for the work of ministry, building up the body of Christ.

children.text: The goal of the gifts: until we attain to the unity of the faith and the knowledge of the Son of God, to mature manhood, the measure of the stature of the fullness of Christ — no longer children tossed by waves, carried about by every wind of doctrine.

Speaking truth in love, we are to grow up into Christ the head — from whom the whole body is joined and held together, each part working properly, making the body grow in love. Growth is simultaneously individual (each part) and corporate (the whole body).

nodes.marker: Now this I say and testify (Τοῦτο οὖν λέγω)
nodes.text: Negatively: no longer walk as the Gentiles walk — futile minds, darkened understanding, alienated from God's life, hardened hearts, callous. Positively: learn Christ — if you have heard him and been taught in him, put off the old self (τὸν παλαιὸν ἄνθρωπον) corrupted by deceitful desires, be renewed in the spirit of your minds, put on the new self (τὸν καινὸν ἄνθρωπον) created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness.

children.text: The three-stage structure (put off / be renewed / put on) is likely baptismal catechesis — clothing imagery (remove old garments, wash, receive new garments) and the new self 'created after the likeness of God' echoes the Genesis creation of humanity in the divine image.

nodes.text: Specific ethical commands flow from the put off/put on framework: speak truth (because we are members of one body); anger without sin (Ps 4:4 — resolve before sundown, give no opportunity to the devil); steal no more but work and give; no corrupting talk but what builds up; do not grieve the Holy Spirit; put away bitterness, wrath, malice. Be kind to one another, tenderhearted, forgiving as God in Christ forgave you.

children.text: Grieving the Spirit: the Spirit who sealed believers for the day of redemption can be grieved by the community's conduct. This gives the ethics a pneumatological gravity — ethical failure is not merely social disruption but an affront to the divine person dwelling within.

discourse.note: Ephesians 4 is the structural pivot of the letter, transitioning from 'you are' to 'therefore walk.' The chapter is unified by the motif of growth — from immature children (v. 14) to the full measure of Christ (v. 13). The five-fold ministry (v. 11) is given not for the leaders' authority but for the equipping of all the saints — leadership as gift to the whole, not position over the whole.

coaching.tip: 'One body, one Spirit, one hope, one Lord, one faith, one baptism, one God and Father' (vv. 4-6). Seven 'ones' forming an early creed. Unity isn't uniformity—gifts differ (v. 11)—but the church shares one foundation. Division denies the oneness Christ died to create.

'Put off your old self... be made new... put on the new self' (vv. 22-24). Clothing imagery: salvation changes what you wear, not just what you believe. The 'old self' isn't reformed but discarded. Christian ethics flows from identity, not willpower.

prayer_prompt: As Paul urges you to live a life worthy of your calling with all humility and gentleness, consider asking God whether there is a gap between the calling He has placed on you and the character you are cultivating.
